meta.title: Claude Builder — 21 Days of AI Building

days.title: Problem Framing
days.concept: Get clear before you build. Fill in sections 1 and 2 of the Build Brief: the problem in two lines, the outcome narrated in five to seven sentences. Then install Claude Desktop and Claude Code and make one file appear using only conversation.
days.deliverable: Build Brief sections 1 and 2 filled in. Claude Desktop and Claude Code installed. A hello-you HTML file open in your browser.
days.idea:
Most people come to Claude with a fuzzy ask and blame the answer. The skill this week is problem framing. Naming who has the problem, what it actually is, and what done looks like before you touch a tool.

Today you do two things. You fill in the first two sections of the Build Brief in plain English. Then with that clarity in hand, you install Claude Desktop and Claude Code, and make one file appear on your screen using only conversation.

Skill clarity. Tool clarity. Both today.
days.howToClaude: I am going to describe a problem in plain language. Help me sharpen it. Who has it. What it actually is. No solution language. Then help me narrate the outcome in five to seven sentences as if it already exists. Walk through what the person does, what they see, what they get.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste the prompt above.
Describe the problem you are circling. Let Claude push back until the statement is in plain human language.
Narrate the outcome in five to seven sentences. Save sections 1 and 2 of your Build Brief.
Install Claude Desktop from claude.ai/download.
Install Claude Code following the prompts. Open it. Type: "Make a file called hello.html that says 'Hi, I am [your name] and I am building this week.' Open it in my browser when done."
days.deploy: Two things in the cohort space: a screenshot of the hello-you file, and your problem statement in two lines.
days.reflection: What got clearer in your problem statement that you had been hiding from yourself?

days.title: Solution Shape
days.concept: Pick the shape your solution takes. Information, workflow, data persistence, or autonomous actions. Name the form it takes. Decide where to build. Sections 3, 4, 5 of the Build Brief.
days.deliverable: Build Brief sections 3, 4, 5 filled in. Shape picked, form named, build surface chosen.
days.idea:
Every solution has a shape. Information, workflow, data persistence, or autonomous actions. The skill today is recognizing which shape your problem actually wants, not which one excites you.

Most people pick the wrong shape because they pick the most interesting one. Claude is the tool that helps you stress-test the choice.

By end of day you will know what you are building, what form it takes, and which Claude surface you will use to build it.
days.howToClaude: Here is my problem and outcome from yesterday: [paste]. Walk me through the four shapes: information, workflow, data persistence, autonomous actions. For each, tell me the test from the Build Brief and ask me to answer honestly. At the end, recommend the shape and explain your reasoning.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste sections 1 and 2 of your Build Brief plus the prompt above.
Answer Claude's questions honestly. Especially the test questions for each shape.
Pick your shape. If two feel right, pick the simpler one.
Write section 4: "My solution is a [shape] that [does what] for [who]."
Write section 5: where you will build. Claude.ai, Project, Cowork, or Claude Code. Start at Claude.ai. Move only if you need to.
days.deploy: Post your one-line form sentence in the cohort. "My solution is a [shape] that [does what] for [who]."
days.reflection: Did you almost pick a more exciting shape that did not match your problem? What pulled you there?

days.title: The Quick Win
days.concept: Write the quick win in one sentence. Build the smallest piece that proves the shape works. Start the iteration log. Foundation is complete.
days.deliverable: Quick win built. Iteration log row one entered.
days.skill: Restraint: building the smallest piece that proves the shape
days.idea:
A quick win is not a full solution. It is the smallest piece that proves the shape works. The skill today is restraint, building exactly what is needed to test the shape and nothing more.

The tool is whichever Claude surface you chose yesterday. The iteration log is the small habit that turns building into a thinking practice.

By end of day you have a working artifact and the first row of your log.
days.howToClaude: Here is my Build Brief so far. Here is my quick win: [one sentence]. Build only that. Single file. Plain. No extras. When done, suggest one small thing I could change tomorrow that would teach me something.
stepByStep: Write section 6 of your Build Brief: one sentence describing the quick win, doable in two hours.
Open the surface you chose yesterday. Paste your brief plus the prompt above.
Let Claude build. Test the result. Use it once.
Open section 7 of your brief, the iteration log. Add row one with today's date, what you built, why, and "yes, it works."
Save everything. Foundation is done.
days.deploy: Post the quick win in the cohort. A screenshot, a URL, or a paragraph if your shape was information.
days.reflection: What did you almost add that you held back on? The Brief works when you can say no to extras.

days.title: Public Voice
days.concept: Write the value copy. Three sentences, then a paragraph. Who, what, why now. Claude.ai sharpens every line until it sounds like a real person, not a marketing site.
days.deliverable: Draft copy for one landing page section: hero headline, subhead, and a single call to action.
days.skill: Clear public voice. Saying what you do in language a stranger can act on.
days.idea:
Now you turn your project outward. The skill is clear public voice: saying what you do and who it is for in language a stranger can act on.

Most landing pages fail because the writer is performing instead of communicating. The tool is Claude.ai, used as a draft partner that pushes you toward shorter, plainer, more honest sentences.

Today is words. Tomorrow you build the page.
days.howToClaude: Here is my Build Brief. I am writing the copy for a landing page. Draft a hero headline (one line), a subhead (one sentence), and one call to action. Make it sound like a real person, not a marketing site. Then ask me three questions that would make it sharper.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste your Build Brief plus the prompt above.
Read Claude's draft out loud. Does it sound like you, or like a template?
Answer the three questions. Let Claude rewrite.
Iterate two or three more rounds until the headline says exactly what you do.
Save the copy in a doc or in your project Notion.
days.deploy: Paste your hero headline and CTA in the cohort. One line each.
days.reflection: What word did Claude suggest that you kept? What word did you replace because it was not yours?

days.title: The Landing Page
days.concept: Build the page in Claude Code. The copy is in your hand. Today is structure, layout, and feel. Lead with feeling, then features.
days.deliverable: Landing page v1 running locally that feels like you, not a template.
days.skill: Translating words and identity into a working page
days.idea:
Today the copy meets the page. The skill is translating words into a layout that respects them. The tool is Claude Code, where you describe the page in plain English and watch structure appear.

The trick is to lead with feeling, not with features. Tell Claude what the page should feel like before you tell it what should be on it.
days.howToClaude: Build me a single-page landing page. Here is the copy: [paste]. I want it to feel [warm, bold, quiet, playful, premium, friendly]. Single HTML file with inline CSS. Mobile first. Clean typography. One section per content block. Keep it simple.
stepByStep: Open Claude Code in a fresh folder for your project.
Paste your copy plus the prompt above with your feeling words.
Let Claude Code build the file. Open it in your browser.
Walk away for ten minutes. Come back fresh. List three things that work and three that are off.
Tell Claude exactly what is off and how you want it instead. Iterate two or three rounds.
days.deploy: Save v1 of the page. Take a screenshot. Compare against the feeling words you started with.
days.reflection: What did you ask for that you could not have specified to a designer six months ago?

days.title: One Action
days.concept: A page that does nothing is a poster. A page that does one thing is a tool. Add one working call to action that completes one real action end to end. One. Not three.
days.deliverable: Page with one CTA that works end to end.
days.skill: Choosing the one action and refusing the rest
days.idea:
A page that does nothing is a poster. A page that does one thing is a tool. The skill today is restraint again: choosing the single action you want a visitor to take and refusing all the others.

The tool is Claude Code, wiring one call to action that actually works end to end. Email capture, calendar link, contact form, a button that does something real. One. Not three.
days.howToClaude: Add a [email capture form using Buttondown, calendar booking link, contact form] to my landing page. When someone uses it, they should [describe what happens]. Keep everything else the same.
stepByStep: Decide the one action before opening Claude Code. Write it down in plain words.
Set up the service that powers the action. Buttondown for email. Cal.com (cal.com) for calendar. Use the free tier.
Copy the embed code or URL from the service.
Open Claude Code with yesterday's project. Paste the prompt above plus any code from the service.
Test the CTA yourself. Submit a real entry. Confirm it lands where it should.
days.deploy: Save the file. Use the CTA one more time as a stranger would.
days.reflection: Did you almost add a second CTA? Why? Notice that pull. It is the pull that kills clarity.

days.title: Go Live
days.concept: Deploy to Netlify. Post the URL in the cohort. Receive feedback as information, not as a verdict on you. First function of value delivered: communicate.
days.deliverable: Landing page live on a Netlify URL, shared in the cohort with three pieces of feedback captured.
days.skill: Shipping and receiving feedback as data
days.idea:
Today the page leaves your computer. The skill is shipping, plus the harder skill of receiving feedback as information instead of as a verdict on you.

The tool is Netlify, the simplest deploy on the internet. Drag your folder and you have a URL. End of week one. One artifact live. One function of value delivered: communicate.
days.howToClaude: You will not need a prompt today. If something breaks, describe what is broken to Claude Code in one sentence and ask for a fix.
stepByStep: Open netlify.com/drop. Drag your project folder.
Wait for the deploy. Copy the URL.
Open the URL on your phone. Confirm it looks right. Use the CTA.
Post the URL in the cohort with one sentence: what is this for, and what kind of feedback would help you most.
Read every reply. Write down the three pieces of feedback that landed, separate from the ones that stung.
days.deploy: The URL in the cohort. Add the URL to your bio or signature today.
days.reflection: What feedback surprised you? Surprise is the data.

days.title: The Candidate
days.concept: Find the recurring thinking problem. The one you solve every week from scratch. Once named, it becomes a candidate for a thinking instrument.
days.deliverable: One paragraph naming the recurring thinking problem, who needs it solved, and what good looks like.
days.skill: Naming a thinking task you do over and over
days.idea:
Today the second function of value: creating value through thinking. The skill is recognizing a thinking task you do over and over and that produces real value when you do it well.

Most people see this work as routine. It is actually a candidate for an instrument. Claude is the tool that turns that recurring thinking into a reusable shape.

Today you name the candidate. The next three days you build the instrument.
days.howToClaude: I want to find a recurring thinking task in my work that I could turn into a reusable Claude instrument. Interview me. Ask one question at a time. Look for: things I think about repeatedly, decisions I make often, structures I rebuild from scratch, briefs I write again and again. Stop when you have three candidates.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste the prompt above.
Answer Claude's questions. Pay attention to "wait, I do that all the time" moments.
Pick the candidate that, if done well every time, would change your week most.
Write one paragraph: the recurring problem, who needs it solved, what good looks like.
Save the paragraph. It is the brief for the rest of the week.
days.deploy: Post the one-paragraph problem in the cohort.
days.reflection: What did you almost dismiss as "just part of the job" that turned out to be a candidate?

days.title: The Shape
days.concept: Design the thinking shape. What inputs go in, what questions get asked, what outputs come out. The shape itself is the value.
days.deliverable: A written design for the thinking shape: inputs, questions, outputs, in one page.
days.skill: Designing the architecture of repeatable thinking
days.idea:
A thinking instrument has a shape. What inputs go in, what questions get asked, what outputs come out. The skill today is designing that shape.

The tool is Claude.ai used not as a chatbot but as a partner sketching the architecture of repeatable thinking. By end of day you have a one-page design that another person could read and understand.
days.howToClaude: Here is the recurring thinking task: [paste yesterday's paragraph]. Help me design the shape. List the inputs that always go in. List the questions that always get asked. List the outputs that always come out. Then suggest the order of the questions. Push back if my list is too long or vague.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste yesterday's paragraph plus the prompt above.
Build the list iteratively. Inputs, questions, outputs.
Test the shape mentally against three real past situations. Does it work for all three?
Tighten. Cut questions that did not earn their place.
Save the design as one page.
days.deploy: Add the one-page design to your project Notion.
days.reflection: Which question almost did not make the list but turned out to matter most?

days.title: The Project
days.concept: Build the Claude Project. Write the custom instructions that encode the shape. Add reference files. Test with a real input. The Project is the artifact.
days.deliverable: A working Claude Project with custom instructions, tested on a real input.
days.skill: Turning a design into a reusable instrument
days.idea:
Today the design becomes a Claude Project. The skill is turning a written design into a working instrument. The tool is the Claude.ai Project feature, with custom instructions that encode the shape and reference files that ground the thinking.

Once built, the Project does the same job the same way every time, available to you whenever you need it.
days.howToClaude: Write custom instructions for a Claude Project that does this: [paste your design]. The instructions should make any new chat in this Project follow the shape automatically. Use clear language. No marketing. Specify what Claude should ask, in what order, and what shape the output should take. Keep it under 300 words.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Create a new Project. Name it after the recurring problem.
Paste the prompt above with your design. Let Claude write the custom instructions.
Paste the instructions into the Project's custom instructions field.
Add any reference files the Project needs (past examples, brand notes, templates).
Open a new chat inside the Project. Test the shape with a real input. Iterate the instructions until the output feels right.
days.deploy: The Project itself is the deliverable. Take a screenshot of the Project page.
days.reflection: What did the Project produce that you would have produced more slowly on your own?

days.title: Prompt Architecture
days.concept: Use the Project for one real situation. Share the Project link with one person who has the same recurring problem. Second function of value delivered: create.
days.deliverable: One real use of the Project documented. Project shared with one person.
days.skill: Putting a thinking instrument into circulation
days.idea: A Project that sits unused is a draft. The skill today is putting the instrument into circulation. Use it once on a real situation, then share it with one other person who has the same recurring problem. The tool is Claude.ai's Project sharing. Week two closes with one new artifact: a thinking instrument you and at least one other person now own.
days.howToClaude: I am about to use this Project for real. Before I start, walk me through what good would look like. After I am done, summarize what we produced and tell me one thing the Project could do better.
stepByStep: Pick the next real situation where the recurring problem shows up. Now, not later.
Open your Project. Paste the prompt above.
Run the situation through the Project. Use the output for real.
Note what worked and what felt off. Edit the custom instructions if needed.
Share the Project link with one person who has the same problem. Tell them the brief.
days.deploy: Post in the cohort: a screenshot or short description of the output, plus who you shared it with.
days.reflection: The Project ran a thinking task while you were doing something else. What does that suggest about the next one you could build?

days.title: The Scope Brief
days.concept: Scope the real build. Ask the one question: what is the simplest version that still solves the problem? Decide what ships, what defers, what is cut entirely.
days.deliverable: A one-page scope brief: problem, primary user, MVP feature list (no more than three), what is deferred.
days.skill: Scoping. Choosing what not to build.
days.idea: The third function of value: delivering a tool that holds state. The skill this week is scoping, the hardest discipline in building. Choosing what not to build. The simplest version that still solves the problem. The tool is Claude.ai for the scoping conversation, then Claude Code for the build. Today is no code. Today is the brief that protects the rest of the week from scope creep.
days.howToClaude: I want to build a small app this week. Here is the problem and the user: [paste]. Help me scope it down. Ask me what the one thing the app must do for someone is. Then write a scope brief: problem, primary user, MVP feature list (no more than three), what is deferred.
stepByStep: Open Claude.ai (claude.ai). Paste your Build Brief plus the prompt above.
Answer Claude's questions honestly. The one-thing question is the most important.
Let Claude draft the scope brief. Read it. Cut anything that is not the one thing.
Show the brief to one person who would use the app. Ask: would you actually use this?
Save the brief. It is the contract for the rest of the week.
days.deploy: Add the scope brief to your project Notion.
days.reflection: What feature did you almost include that you now realize is for someone you are not building for?
